coredata.dc:title: Mechanism study of Dual-Emission ratiometric fluorescent pH-Sensitive carbon quantum dots and its application on mornitoring enzymatic catalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Zhiyu
Ning, De
Jia, Kaihong
Liu, Hao
Xiang, Yong
Cao, Jinlong
Chen, Junxian
Zhong, Yeshuang
Wang, Xinyu
Zhang, Zhen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CQD) have received significant attention as a novel ratiometric fluorescent pH nanoprobe, owing to their favorable optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. Despite their appealing features, the precise mechanism behind the pH-sensitive photoluminescence of CQDs remains to be fully understood. This study endeavors to unravel the mechanism underlying the pH-responsive ratiometric fluorescence in dual-emission CQDs, synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and oxalic acid as precursors. The resultant CQDs exhibit inherent dual-emission at wavelengths of 383 nm and 566 nm, with the ratiometric fluorescence response tailored by the ratio of precursors, providing a robust tool for pH sensing across a range of 2 to 6. Detailed characterizations, including chemical, morphological, and optical analyses, alongside theoretical insights from time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT), elucidate the mechanism underlying the pH-dependent luminescence, attributed to the electron cloud transmission between amide and adjacent carboxyl groups on the CQD surface. The superior performance of these CQDs in real-time pH monitoring is demonstrated through their application in glucose oxidase-catalyzed reactions, showcasing their potential as efficient, reliable nanoprobes for biomedical research and diagnostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent pH sensing
pH nanoprobe
Glucose catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reversibility and stability of pH response
Reversibility and stability test of the DECQDs for pH detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The high-resolution XPS spectra of (a) DECQD-1, (b) DECQD-2, (c) DECQD-3, and (d) DECQD-4 samples for their C 1 s signal.
(a) The TEM image of DECQDs (inset shows size distribution of CQDs in the sample); (b) High-resolution TEM image of a typical DECQD in (a).
Emission spectra of (a) DECQD-1, (b) DECQD-2, (c) DECQD-3, and (d) DECQD-4 under different excitation wavelength.

(a, d, g, j) pH-dependent emission spectra for DECQD-1, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4, respectively. (b, e, h, k) Relationship between bimodal ratio variation and pH value for DECQD-1, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4, respectively. (c, f, i, l) Optical images under visible light and 365 nm UV light for DECQD-1, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4, respectively.
（a）The peak intensity of 383 and 566 nm for DECQD-2 with pH value varied repeatedly between 3.0 and 7.0. (b) Fluorescence spectra of DECQD-2 samples within three weeks.
The corresponding dominant NTO pairs and fluorescence of (a) DECQD-1, (b) DECQD-2, (c)DECQD-3, (d)DECQD-4 with the S1 and S3 excited states.

The pH changes during glucose-catalyzed reactions process monitored by our pH-sensitive fluorescent CQDs. (a) The pH change of glucose catalytic contrast test; (b) The change of fluorescence intensity bimodal ratio in the glucose-DECQDs catalytic process; (c) The optical image of glucose-catalyzed reaction process from 0 to 3 h, mornitored by DECQD-2 sample under visible light and UV light irradiation.
Some reported research of pH fluorescent nanoprobes.

head.ce:title: Mechanism study of Dual-Emission ratiometric fluorescent pH-Sensitive carbon quantum dots and its application on mornitoring enzymatic catalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CQD) have received significant attention as a novel ratiometric fluorescent pH nanoprobe, owing to their favorable optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. Despite their appealing features, the precise mechanism behind the pH-sensitive photoluminescence of CQDs remains to be fully understood. This study endeavors to unravel the mechanism underlying the pH-responsive ratiometric fluorescence in dual-emission CQDs, synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and oxalic acid as precursors. The resultant CQDs exhibit inherent dual-emission at wavelengths of 383 nm and 566 nm, with the ratiometric fluorescence response tailored by the ratio of precursors, providing a robust tool for pH sensing across a range of 2 to 6. Detailed characterizations, including chemical, morphological, and optical analyses, alongside theoretical insights from time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT), elucidate the mechanism underlying the pH-dependent luminescence, attributed to the electron cloud transmission between amide and adjacent carboxyl groups on the CQD surface. The superior performance of these CQDs in real-time pH monitoring is demonstrated through their application in glucose oxidase-catalyzed reactions, showcasing their potential as efficient, reliable nanoprobes for biomedical research and diagnostic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The precise quantification of pH levels holds paramount importance across the biomedical landscape, where the subtle shifts in acidity and alkalinity are pivotal in the intricate ballet of cellular processes [1]. Within eukaryotic organisms, the regulation of pH spans a vast continuum, from the molecular intricacies of metabolic pathways [2] to the broader physiological scales of protein conformation, organellar function, and systemic tissue health. Such regulation becomes especially critical in the context of disease pathogenesis, where aberrant pH dynamics are hallmarks of tumorigenesis [3], wound healing [4], and inflammatory responses within the esophageal milieu [5]. Given this backdrop, the quest for precise and reliable pH monitoring methodologies has emerged as a field of significant scientific inquiry.

Among the spectrum of pH sensing technologies, fluorescent-based pH sensors, particularly organic dyes [6–10], have demonstrated considerable sensitivity and adaptability for intracellular applications. However, their utility is marred by susceptibility to environmental variables and a propensity for photobleaching, factors which severely compromise their biological applicability. In this context, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) have ascended as a beacon of potential, heralded for their stellar optical properties, enduring photostability, non-toxicity, aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility. Furthermore, the facile tunability of their surface chemistry enables pH-responsive luminescent behavior through the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups, positioning CQDs as a prime candidate for biomedicine's pH monitoring arsenal [11–14]. At the same time, the carbon quantum dots are enhanced and quenched by coupling with metal ions. Therefore, it has the function of detecting ion concentration, which is also widely concerned [15–17].

The literature abounds with explorations into CQDs’ pH-sensitive prowess. Notably, research highlighting single-emission CQDs, which exhibit heightened pH responsivity within the physiological window, underscores their utility in wound pH monitoring [18]. Meanwhile, the work of Wolfgang et al., showcasing white light-emitting CQDs capable of dual emission, brings to light the limitations of dye-assisted fluorescence, pointing towards the necessity for intrinsically ratiometric and self-calibrating fluorescent pH probes [19]. In this vein, Xia et al. have pioneered the development of dual-emission CQDs, employing o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and oxalic acid (OA) as precursors, to unveil CQDs that exhibit pH-correlated fluorescence ratios, heralding a new era of ratiometric luminescent nanoprobes for pH detection [14]. In this paper, some of the reported literature on pH fluorescent nanoprobes have been investigated and sorted out, as shown in the Table 1 . Despite these advancements, a comprehensive elucidation of the dual photoluminescent signals and their pH-responsive emission mechanisms remains elusive, a gap that this study aims to bridge.

This work delves into the mechanistic underpinnings of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence exhibited by CQDs, synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method leveraging varying ratios of o-PD [27] and OA [28] as reactants. The resultant CQDs exhibit intrinsic dual-emission, displaying a unique ratiometric fluorescence response to pH variations within the range of 2 to 6. Through a combination of chemical and optical characterizations, alongside time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) analysis, we attribute the dual-emission phenomenon to electron cloud movement between amide groups and adjacent carboxyl groups, a process dictated by the choice of reactants. This ratiometric fluorescence is ascribed to distinct electron cloud transmission modes, varying with the excitation state and being modulated by the pH through reversible activation and deactivation of surface carboxyl groups. The culmination of this study is the identification of dual emission CQDs (DECQD) with optimal pH-sensitive fluorescence properties, earmarked for pioneering applications in monitoring enzymatic catalysis within the biomedical domain.

Our investigation into the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent pH response of CQDs began with the synthesis of four distinct sets of CQD samples, differentiated by their respective reactant ratios. Utilizing o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the foundational carbon and nitrogen source, we leveraged its benzene-amino structure for efficient CQD formation. The selection of oxalic acid (OA) as the oxygen source, characterized by its dual carboxyl groups, aimed to enhance the oxygen-carbon ratio within the CQDs. This strategic choice of reactants culminated in the creation of four samples, termed DECQD-1 through DECQD-4, synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The reactant ratios ranged from 2.8 g o-PD with incremental additions of OA from 0 g to 3 g.

As for the precursor in the reactants, the carbon quantum dots were purified by filtration and dialysis. First, after the hydrothermal reaction, we removed the solid reactants from the carbon quantum dot solution by a filtration step. Furthermore, the dialysis method is adopted to clean the carbon quantum dot solution, thus it can be considered that the precursor of o-PD and OA are removed in the dialysis step.

To dissect the influence of OA on the electronic and chemical architecture of CQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) offered detailed insights, as shown in Fig. S1. The calibration of binding energy, using C 1 s at 284.8 eV as a benchmark, revealed prominent peaks at 286.3 eV (C 1 s), 399.6 eV (N 1 s), and 533.6 eV (O 1 s) across all samples [29], signaling the successful doping of nitrogen and oxygen. Notably, an additional peak at 497.1 eV emerged under neutral pH conditions, attributed to Na+ incorporation and subsequent carboxyl group deprotonization—a phenomenon reversed in acidic environments due to protonation. This observation aligns with prior research indicating that Na's incorporation does not perturb the fluorescence properties of the CQDs [42].

High-resolution XPS spectra further delineated the chemical composition of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , the C 1 s spectrum presented four distinct Gaussian-fitted peaks corresponding to various bonds: C = C, C-O/C-N, and C = O [30]. In the case of DECQD-1, the absence of OA during synthesis resulted in negligible oxygen introduction, as evidenced by a minimal peak at 287.8 eV, consistent with a lack of oxygen-containing functional groups, as shown in Fig. 1(a). The oxygen-containing functional group is hypothesized and verified to be carboxyl group in the computational section. This sample primarily exhibited C = C and C-N bonds, reflective of the o-PD reactant's role in CQD and nitrogen-containing functional group synthesis. The nitrogen-containing functional group is also hypothesized and verified to be amide group in the computational section. Conversely, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4 demonstrated a marked increase in the 287.8 eV peak intensity, indicative of successful carboxyl group integration, as shown in Fig. 1(b), 1(c), and 1(d), respectively. This intensity correlated directly with the amount of OA used, suggesting that higher OA concentrations facilitate the formation of additional carboxyl groups on the CQD surface.

The structural intricacies of the as-synthesized dual-emission Carbon Quantum Dots (DECQDs) were examined through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), a pivotal step in delineating the physicochemical underpinnings of their fluorescent capabilities. The HRTEM analysis unveiled a heterogeneous distribution of CQD sizes within the solution, with instances of particle coalescence attributed to the elevated surface activation energy inherent to these nanostructures. This phenomenon was graphically represented in Fig. 2 (a), where an aggregation of 75 CQDs furnished an average diameter of 5.63 nm, spanning a spectrum from 1 to 10 nm.

Further HRTEM inspection, as showcased in Fig. 2(b), brought to light the crystalline demeanor of a typical DECQD, measuring approximately 5.90 nm in diameter. This observation was underscored by the discernible lattice spacing of 0.235 nm, aligning with the (002) crystal face characteristic of bulk graphite, as corroborated by preceding scholarly endeavors [14]. The revelation of this lattice spacing is not merely a structural identifier but signifies the single crystalline nature of the synthesized CQDs.

This lattice and morphological scrutiny not only reaffirms the successful synthesis of nano-sized, graphite-structured CQDs but also highlights their uniformity in size distribution—a critical attribute for the efficacy of ratiometric fluorescent probes. The elucidation of these structural nuances plays a foundational role in understanding the observed ratiometric fluorescent behavior, paving the way for the precise application of DECQDs in pH monitoring and beyond.

An exhaustive investigation into the luminescence profiles of the DECQDs was conducted, leveraging both fluorescence excitation and emission spectra to unravel the intricacies of their optical behavior. The fluorescence emission spectra, spanning excitation wavelengths from 340 to 440 nm, were meticulously analyzed to ascertain the presence of characteristic emission peaks at 383 nm and 566 nm for the DECQD samples, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The excitation spectra pertinent to these emission wavelengths highlighted a pronounced peak at approximately 360 nm across all DECQD variations, corroborating the findings presented in Supplementary Fig. S2.

Notably, the 383 nm emission intensity from the DECQD-1 sample exhibited negligible presence in acidic conditions, as delineated in Fig. 3(a). This contrasted starkly with the observable emission peaks at 383 nm in samples DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4, thereby suggesting that the 383 nm emission is inherently tied to the the carboxyl groups adorning the CQD surfaces. Among the variants, DECQD-2 demonstrated the most pronounced emission intensity at this wavelength, with subsequent reductions observed for DECQD-3 and DECQD-4, as depicted in Fig. 3(b)-(d). This trend implies a direct correlation between the emission intensity at 383 nm and the concentration of surface-bound carboxyl groups, delineating a nuanced interplay between the structural and optical properties of the DECQDs.

Conversely, the emission intensities at 566 nm displayed minimal variance across the four DECQD formulations, indicating a uniform excitation mechanism for this emission wavelength among the samples. This observation underscores a pivotal distinction in the photoluminescent pathways activated within the DECQDs, with the 383 nm emissions primarily influenced by surface chemistry modifications, whereas the 566 nm emissions appear resilient to such variations. The fluorescence spectra of the four samples were similar to the quantum efficiency measurements shown in Table S1, the quantum efficiencies of DECQD-2, DECQD-3 and DECQD-4 with OA as O source were 11.07 %, 42.60 % and 22.73 %, respectively, which were much higher than those of DECQD-1 of 0.2 %. he results show that the samples of DECQD-2、DECQD-3、DECQD-4 have good luminescent properties due to the introduction of OA as O source.

The core of our investigation into the dual-emission Carbon Quantum Dots (DECQDs) centered on their ability to function as ratiometric pH sensors. This capability was evaluated by recording the fluorescence spectra of each DECQD variant across a pH range of 2 to 8, under a consistent excitation wavelength of 365 nm, as meticulously documented in Fig. 4 . All samples distinctly manifested dual emission peaks at 383 nm and 566 nm. The intensity variations of these peaks with pH adjustments are presented for DECQD-1, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4 in Fig. 4(a), 4(d), 4(g), and 4(j), respectively.

DECQD-1 displayed relatively muted emission at 383 nm across the studied pH range, attributed to a deficiency in oxygen source during synthesis, which in turn limited carboxyl group formation crucial for luminescence at 383 nm. In contrast, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4 showed markedly stronger 383 nm emissions, underscoring the pivotal role of carboxyl groups in this photoluminescent process. Notably, the 383 nm emission intensity inversely correlated with pH values, whereas the 566 nm emission remained consistent across all samples, reaffirming earlier observations that the excitation mechanism at 566 nm is independent of carboxyl group presence.

To quantitatively assess the pH-responsive ratiometric fluorescence, we introduced a bimodal ratio Δ (Δ = I383/I566), representing the intensity ratio of the 383 nm to 566 nm emissions. This ratio, reflecting the logarithmic variation of OH–/H+ concentration, was empirically modeled to derive the pH sensitivity and linearity of each DECQD sample. DECQD-1 exhibited a linear decrease in Δ with rising pH (2–6), achieving a linearity of 95.85 % (R2) and a sensitivity coefficient of 0.66. DECQD-2, with its optimal carboxyl group content, demonstrated superior sensitivity (0.78) and a high linearity of 96.61 % within the same pH range, as detailed in Fig. 4(e). Meanwhile, DECQD-3 and DECQD-4 also displayed a decreasing Δ trend with increasing pH, albeit with sensitivities of 0.55 and 0.43, respectively, and linearity values of 97.75 % and 94.29 %, as seen in Fig. 4(h) and 4(k). The diminishing sensitivity across these samples is attributed to the potential inhibitory effect of excess carboxyl groups on the 383 nm emission—a hypothesis further explored subsequently.

Photoluminescence (PL) emission vividness under acidic conditions and UV irradiation for each sample variant is visually captured in Fig. 4(c), 4(f), 4(i), and 4(l), demonstrating the practical utility of DECQDs as pH sensors in real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility and stability of pH response
ce:section.ce:para: A paramount attribute for any sensor, especially those deployed in dynamic biological environments, is the ability to consistently revert to its baseline state upon the reversal of the sensing condition. This reversibility was stringently assessed for the DECQD-2 sample, chosen for its exemplary sensitivity and linearity in pH sensing. The experiment was conducted by cyclically adjusting the solution's pH between 3 and 7, followed by recording of the emission intensities at each pH level.

The emission intensities exhibited negligible variance upon successive cycles between the pH extremes, demonstrating the DECQD-2 sample's reversible pH response, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). This consistency not only attests to the robustness of the CQDs but also heralds their potential as high-fidelity nanoprobes for biological and chemical analyses.

The fluorescence spectrum of DECQD-2 sample is also measured over a 3-week period, as shown in Fig. 5(b). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of DECQD-2 sample has no obvious change in three weeks. The stability of the prepared carbon quantum dots can be proved.

Given its outstanding performance in terms of sensitivity, linearity, and reversibility, DECQD-2 emerges as the ideal candidate for further exploration as a ratiometric pH-dependent fluorescent nanoprobe. Its deployment in the monitoring of enzymatic catalysis represents a pioneering application, offering a glimpse into the vast potential of DECQDs in the realms of biomedicine and environmental sensing.

To dissect the dual-emission mechanism inherent to CQDs, a computational approach leveraging the density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) was adopted. This theoretical framework aimed to simulate fluorescence emissions, particularly spotlighting specific surface functional groups conjectured to significantly influence these emissions. Predicated on the presence of C = O and C-N bonds, carboxyl and amide groups were posited as the key functional entities facilitating dual-emission wavelengths at 383 nm and 566 nm, respectively. Our computational findings suggest that the dual-emission phenomenon can be primarily attributed to the electron cloud dynamics between amide groups and their adjacent carboxyl counterparts. Specifically, emissions at 566 nm were aligned with the S0 → S1 transition, while the 383 nm emissions corresponded to the S0 → S3 transition. To further elucidate the electron transfer mechanisms, Natural Transition Orbitals (NTO) analysis was employed, revealing nuanced insights into the electron cloud transmission styles influenced by the surface functional group composition, as shown in Fig. 6 .

For the DECQD-1 sample, characterized by the absence of oxalic acid (OA) and consequently, a scarcity of carboxyl groups, the electron transition during excitation predominantly followed a local excitation (LE) pattern, resulting in subdued 383 nm emission intensity, as shown in Fig. 6(a). In contrast, the introduction of a precise quantity of OA in the DECQD-2 synthesis facilitated a robust electron transfer, manifesting as charge transfer (CT) transitions, as shown in Fig. 6(b). This adjustment markedly amplified the 383 nm emission intensity. Further augmentation of OA quantities for DECQD-3 and DECQD-4 led to an over-saturation of carboxyl groups, impeding the electron cloud pathway between amide and adjacent carboxyl groups and reverting the transmission style back to LE, as shown in Fig. 6(c) and 6(d). Consequently, an increase in carboxyl group concentration inversely affected the 383 nm emission intensity. Notably, the S0 → S1 transitions across all samples predominantly exhibited LE characteristics, maintaining consistent emission intensities at 566 nm, in stark alignment with experimental observations. Notably, the above mentioned the nature of S0 → S1 and S0 → S3 excitation are π → π*.

This theoretical exploration also addressed the pH-sensitive photoluminescent (PL) behavior of CQDs, attributing it to the protonation and deprotonization of surface carboxyl groups in response to pH variations. The activation of carboxyl groups from −COONa to –COOH under decreasing pH conditions enhances their participation in electron cloud transitions, thereby elevating the 383 nm emission intensity—a trend that inversely correlates with pH elevation.

To explore the utility of our pH-sensitive fluorescent CQDs in biologically relevant settings, we conducted an in vitro experiment designed to emulate the physicochemical reactions within organisms that induce substantial pH alterations. This involved monitoring the pH shifts during the glucose oxidase-catalyzed oxidation of glucose—a reaction that generates gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide, leading to a decrease in solution pH. The experimental setup consisted of a 50 mL aqueous glucose solution, into which 100 μL of glucose oxidase solution was introduced. This addition precipitated a notable pH reduction from an initial 7.21 to 3.13, with the reaction system reaching equilibrium after approximately 2.5 h. To assess the potential influence of buffer systems on this reaction, 100 μL of a 0.1 M PBS buffer was also incorporated, revealing minimal impact on the reaction dynamics.

Throughout the catalysis process, the photoluminescent (PL) properties of the CQDs were periodically measured, yielding logarithmic intensity ratios (log(Δ = I383/I566)) of −0.959, −0.781, and 0.968 at 0.5, 2.5, and 19.5 h, respectively. These values correspond closely with pH levels of 5.55, 3.32, and 3.08, corroborating the accuracy of pH-meter readings, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). Fig. 7(b) graphically represents these PL characteristics over the duration of the catalytic process.

Furthermore, the qualitative potential of this approach for pH analysis was demonstrated through the optical visualization of the reaction mixture. As depicted in Fig. 7(c), discernible changes in the PL color of the solution were observable over time, attributed to the ratiometric fluorescence property of the CQDs. To further quantify the fluorescence changes in the Glucose catalytic process under DECQDs labeling, the fluorescence emission spectra of Aqueous, PBS and Glucose Oxidase at 0, 0.5, and 3 h have been tested, as shown in Fig. S3. This visual transition not only underscores the sensitivity of the CQDs to pH fluctuations but also highlights their potential for real-time, non-invasive monitoring of enzymatic reactions, which thereby offers valuable insights into dynamic biochemical processes.

In this study, we have meticulously delineated the development and characterization of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent CQDs for precise pH measurement, with a particular focus on the mechanism of ratiometric pH-sensitive fluorescence and monitoring enzymatic catalysis in biomedical applications. Our comprehensive investigation commenced with the synthesis of CQDs using a one-step hydrothermal method, employing o-PD and OA in varying ratios. This approach yielded CQDs exhibiting intrinsic dual-emission properties, attributed to the electron cloud transmission between amide and adjacent carboxyl groups on the CQD surface. The ratiometric fluorescence behavior of these CQDs, responsive to pH changes, was thoroughly examined through chemical, morphological, and optical characterizations, alongside theoretical analyses utilizing time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT). The ratiometric pH sensing capability of the CQDs was rigorously evaluated, demonstrating high sensitivity, linearity, and reversibility across a pH range of 2 to 6. This variant's effectiveness was further validated in a practical application scenario, monitoring the glucose oxidase-catalyzed conversion of glucose, where it accurately reflected the pH changes associated with the enzymatic reaction. This work not only paves the way for the development of next-generation fluorescent nanoprobes but also highlights the versatility of CQDs in biosensing and nanomedicine.

O-phenylenediamine (o-PD), oxalic acid (OA), dibasic sodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), and citric acid (CA) are purchased from Aladdin. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution (10×, pH = 7.3–7.5) are purchased from Aladdin. Glucose and deionized water were purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Glucose oxidase (158.523U/mg) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals and reagents were used without further purification..

The dual-emission CQDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal route using o-PD and OA as the reactants. Four parallel CQDs groups, named DECQD-1, DECQD-2, DECQD-3, and DECQD-4, were fabricated using a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method with a proportion of o-PD to OA of 2.8 g:0 g, 2.8 g:1 g, 2.8 g:2 g, and 2.8 g:3 g, respectively. For each group, the reactants were dissolved in 70 mL DI water. After 30 min of ultrasonic treatment, the solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflonlined stainless steel reactor, heated to 180 ˚C for 10 h in an isothermal oven. The DECQDs solution is then collected after slowly cooled down to room temperature.

In the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), o-phenylenediamine and oxalic acid are typically converted through a series of chemical reactions under high temperature and pressure conditions. The process involves:
1. Carbonization: The precursors decompose and form carbon nuclei.
2. Nucleation: These carbon nuclei aggregate to form small particles.
3. Growth: The particles grow into nanometer-sized carbon dots.

During the synthesis, the o-phenylenediamine serves as a carbon source, and oxalic acid acts as an oxidizing agent, facilitating the formation of carbon dots by promoting the dehydration and condensation reactions.

The specific conversion can be described as follows:
1.
Oxidation of o-phenylenediamine: o-Phenylenediamine undergoes oxidation, and the carbon atoms form the core structure of the CQDs.
2.
Reduction of oxalic acid: Oxalic acid gets reduced, releasing CO2 and H2O, which contribute to the formation of the CQD surface functional groups.

The overall reaction mechanism involves the simultaneous reduction of oxalic acid and oxidation of o-phenylenediamine, leading to the formation of CQDs with various surface functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, which enhance their solubility and luminescent properties.

After the hydrothermal reaction, the prepared CQDs solution was further purified by filtration and dialysis. First, a tubular filter (0.22 μm was used to filter the CQDs solution, and the filtered solution was collected. Further, The dialysis of the CQDs solution was conducted using a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 3600 Da) for 36 h (changing deionized water every 6 h) [31].

The pH buffer solution for adjusting DECQDs during optical characterizations with different pH value was prepared with 0.1 M CA and 0.2 M Na2HPO4, according to the sodium phosphate dibasic-citric acid buffer standard formula table. The solution with various pH were obtained by dispersing prepared DECQDs solution in the abovementioned pH buffer solution with a volume ratio of 1:10. The fluorescence spectra of the suspensions (lex = 365 nm) were obtained after equilibration of 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversibility and stability test of the DECQDs for pH detection
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the DECQD solution was adjusted to 3.0 and 7.0 for 3 cycles by adding 1 M H3PO4 and NaOH, respectively. Then, the fluorescence spectra of each pH point were obtained at lex = 365 nm. The fluorescence spectra of DECQD-2 samples were recorded every other week for three weeks.

A solution of glucose oxidase (20 mg/mL) was prepared in purchased PBS solution (10×, pH = 7.3–7.5) at a concentration of 100 mM and at ambient temperature. Subsequently, 100 μL of glucose oxidase solution was added to a 50 mL glucose aqueous solution (20 mM), and the pH of the system was measured over a certain period of time. Two control groups were established in parallel: one consisted of only 50 mL glucose aqueous solution (20 mM) without glucose oxidase or PBS buffer, while the other group contained 50 mL glucose aqueous solution (20 mM) with the addition of 100 μL PBS buffer (100 mM). Afterwards, a 5 mL solution of CQDs was added to the 50 mL glucose aqueous solution containing glucose catalytic enzyme, and the fluorescence changes of the system were monitored under a 365 nm UV lamp at 30-minute intervals.

In this study, various analytical techniques were employed to characterize DECQDs and investigate their morphology, elemental composition, chemical environment, functional groups, photoluminescence, and absorption properties. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, shimadzu Inc, Axis Supra, Japan) with 150 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) was used to determine the elemental composition and chemical environments of the CQDs. The XPS sample was freeze-dried CQDs powder. Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR, Bruker Optics Inc, INVENIO R, USA) was employed to identify the functional groups of the CQDs. The Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, FEI NanoPorts, Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin TMP, USA) was used to observe the morphology and size distribution of the CQDs. The photoluminescence(PL) and absorption properties of the DECQDs were investigated using fluorescence spectrophotometry (HORIBA Scientific, FluoroMax-4) and UV–vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus), respectively. Plagiarism was avoided by appropriately paraphrasing the original content and citing the sources of the techniques used.

A series of DECQD structures have been constructed. Based on changes in oxalic acid content during the preparation process, amide groups and different numbers of carboxyl functional groups was considered the important roles to lead to variations in the fluorescence spectrum. In order to explore the fluorescence emission mechanism, carboxyl functional groups with a content of 1,3,6,7 are connected to carbon quantum dots containing amide groups. Recently, time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) and PCM were utilized for excited-state optimization with the inclusion of bulk solvent effects [32–34]. Numerous computational studies using PCM/TD-DFT have demonstrated favorable outcomes when compared with experimental values [35–37]. Consequently, the PCM/TD-DFT methodology is employed to investigate the intricate optical properties of DECQDs characterized by varying numbers of carboxyl groups.

The ground-state geometry optimizations of DECQDs are initially computed using Density Functional Theory (DFT) employing the Becke three-parameter Lee–Yang–Parr hybrid functional (B3LYP) in conjunction with a 6-31G* basis set (B3LYP/6-31G*) [38]. After obtaining the optimized structure, the TD-DFT calculations are performed to obtain fluorescence spectrum of DECQDs in the optimized ground state geometry with the same level of theory. Thus, all calculations in this work are conducted for DEGQDs in water. The solvent was included based on the PCM. For both calculations, quantum chemical modules implemented in Gaussian16 software are employed [39]. On the basis of the results of TD-DFT calculation, Natural Transition Orbitals (NTO) analysis was performed by the Multiwfn software [40]. The molecular configurations were visualized using the VMD 1.9.1 program [41].

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by West China Hospital of Sichuan University-The University of Electronic Science and Technology of China Medical and Industrial Integration Cross-Cultivation Project (ZYG-X2022YGRH006); Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Superhard Materials (China Nonferrous Metal Guilin Research Institute of Geology for Mineral Resources) (2023-K-01); the Sichuan Province Science and Technology Activities Funding for Returned Overseas Scholars.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125048.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
